content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of length NNN.

Let F(A)F(A)F(A) denote the maximum frequency of any element present in the array.

For example:


If A=[1,2,3,2,2,1]A = [1, 2, 3, 2, 2, 1]A=[1,2,3,2,2,1], then F(A)=3F(A) = 3F(A)=3 since element 222 has the highest frequency =3 = 3=3.
If A=[1,2,3,3,2,1]A = [1, 2, 3, 3, 2, 1]A=[1,2,3,3,2,1], then F(A)=2F(A) = 2F(A)=2 since highest frequency of any element is 222.
Chef can perform the following operation **at most once**:


Choose any subsequence SSS of the array such that every element of SSS is the same, say xxx. Then, choose an integer yyy and replace every element in this subsequence with yyy.
For example, let A=[1,2,2,1,2,2]A = [1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2]A=[1,2,2,1,2,2]. A few examples of the operation that Chef can perform are:


[1,2,2,1,2,2]→[1,5,5,1,2,2][1, \textcolor{red}{2, 2}, 1, 2, 2] \to [1, \textcolor{blue}{5, 5}, 1, 2, 2][1,2,2,1,2,2]→[1,5,5,1,2,2]
[1,2,2,1,2,2]→[1,19,2,1,19,19][1, \textcolor{red}{2}, 2, 1, \textcolor{red}{2, 2}] \to [1, \textcolor{blue}{19}, 2, 1, \textcolor{blue}{19, 19}][1,2,2,1,2,2]→[1,19,2,1,19,19]
[1,2,2,1,2,2]→[2,2,2,1,2,2][\textcolor{red}{1}, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2] \to [\textcolor{blue}{2}, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2][1,2,2,1,2,2]→[2,2,2,1,2,2]
Determine the **minimum** possible value of F(A)F(A)F(A) Chef can get by performing the given operation at most once.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN denoting the length of array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output the minimum value of F(A)F(A)F(A) Chef can get if he performs the operation optimally.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with integer N, the length of the array.
- A line with N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
All Ai satisfy 1 ≤ Ai ≤ N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum possible value of F(A) after performing at most one allowed operation.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: frequencies are {1:2,2:2}. max_f=2, max2=2, answer=max(2,1)=2.\nTest case 2: only value 1 with frequency 5. max_f=5, max2=0, answer=max(0,3)=3.\nTest case 3: frequencies {1:2,2:4}. max_f=4, max2=2, answer=max(2,2)=2.

hidden.label: edge case – all same with even count
general case with two dominant values
general case with many distinct values

solution.approach:
For each test case count the frequency of every distinct value. Let max_f be the largest frequency and max2 the second largest (0 if only one distinct value exists).
If we choose the value with frequency max_f and change a part of its occurrences to a brand new value, the two resulting frequencies are k and max_f‑k. The best split is k = ⌈max_f/2⌉, giving a maximum frequency of ⌈max_f/2⌉ for this value. All other values keep their original frequencies, the largest among them being max2. Therefore after the optimal operation the answer is max(max2, ⌈max_f/2⌉). If we do not perform any operation the answer would be max_f, which is never smaller than the expression above, so the formula already gives the minimal possible value.
Algorithm per test case:
1. Count frequencies using a hash map.
2. Find the largest (max_f) and second largest (max2) frequencies.
3. Answer = max(max2, (max_f + 1) / 2).
Complexities: O(N) time and O(distinct values) memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the array. Overall O(ΣN) ≤ 2·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(D) where D is the number of distinct values (≤ N).
